title: Reliability, Validity & Research Ethics
simple_html:
After you collect data, two questions decide whether anyone should trust it: Is your measurement consistent (reliability)? And does it measure the right thing (validity)? On top of that, research must be done ethically — protecting the people who give you their data.

Reliability — consistency

Reliability means you get the same result if you measure again under the same conditions. A bathroom scale that shows a different weight each time you step on it is unreliable. In research, we check whether survey items hold together (internal consistency) and whether results repeat over time (test–retest).

Validity — accuracy

Validity means your instrument actually captures the concept you intended. A scale can be perfectly consistent yet wrong — if it always reads 5kg too heavy, it is reliable but not valid. Validity asks: am I measuring what I claim to measure, and are my conclusions justified?

Reliability vs validity

The classic image is a dartboard. Reliable but not valid = tight cluster off the bullseye. Valid but not reliable = scattered around the centre. You want both: a tight cluster on the bullseye. Reliability is necessary but not sufficient for validity.

Research ethics

Core principles: informed consent (people agree, knowing what's involved), no harm, confidentiality/anonymity, voluntary participation, and honest reporting. Most studies need ethics-committee approval before any data is collected.

Key takeaways

  • Reliability = consistent; validity = accurate.
  • Reliability is required for validity, but does not guarantee it.
  • Protect participants: consent, confidentiality, no harm.
  • Get ethics approval before you start.
intermediate_html:
Reliability, validity, and ethics are the quality-and-conscience controls of empirical research. Reliability and validity determine whether your evidence can be believed; ethics determines whether it should have been gathered at all. MBA examiners frequently test the distinction between these concepts, so precision matters.

Reliability: consistency of measurement

Reliability is the extent to which a measure yields stable, consistent results. The main types are: test–retest (same instrument, same people, different time — do scores correlate?); internal consistency (do items on a scale measure the same construct? — assessed with Cronbach's alpha, where ≥ 0.70 is generally acceptable); and inter-rater reliability (do different observers agree? — e.g. Cohen's kappa). Reliability is necessary but not sufficient for validity.

Validity: accuracy of measurement and inference

TypeQuestion it answers
Content validityDo the items cover the full concept?
Construct validityDoes the instrument capture the abstract concept?
Criterion validityDoes it correlate with an external benchmark?
Internal validityIs the cause–effect claim justified (no confounds)?
External validityDo findings generalise beyond the sample?

Construct validity has two halves: convergent (correlates with measures it should) and discriminant (does not correlate with unrelated measures).

The dartboard analogy

Reliability without validity = consistently hitting the same wrong spot. Validity without reliability is logically near-impossible — you cannot consistently hit the target if your shots scatter. So: establish reliability first, then validity.

Qualitative quality criteria

Because reliability/validity are rooted in quantitative thinking, Lincoln & Guba (1985) propose parallel trustworthiness criteria: credibility (≈ internal validity), transferability (≈ external validity), dependability (≈ reliability), and confirmability (≈ objectivity). Techniques include triangulation, member checking, and audit trails.

Research ethics

Key principles: informed consent, voluntary participation and right to withdraw, avoidance of harm (physical, psychological, reputational), confidentiality and anonymity, data protection (e.g. GDPR), and integrity (no fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism). Studies normally require Institutional Review Board / ethics committee approval before fieldwork.

Worked example

You build a 10-item "manager trust" scale. Cronbach's alpha = 0.88 (good reliability). You check content validity with an expert panel, then test construct validity: it correlates 0.6 with a known engagement scale (convergent) and only 0.1 with an unrelated numeracy test (discriminant). Before any of this, you secured ethics approval, obtained signed informed consent, anonymised responses, and stored data securely. How to apply it: report reliability and validity evidence explicitly in your methods chapter — examiners and reviewers expect the numbers, not just claims.
full_html:
Reliability, validity, and ethics constitute the credibility framework of empirical research. Reliability and validity answer the epistemic question — can we believe these findings? — while ethics answers the moral question — was this research conducted responsibly? Mastery requires both the precise psychometric distinctions and an appreciation of how the criteria translate across the quantitative–qualitative divide.

Reliability: the consistency of measurement

Reliability is the degree to which a measurement procedure produces stable and consistent results across repetitions (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019). In classical test theory, an observed score is the sum of a true score and random error; reliability is the proportion of variance attributable to the true score. The principal forms are: test–retest reliability (temporal stability — correlate scores from the same respondents at two times); parallel-forms reliability (equivalence of two versions of an instrument); internal consistency, the most reported in business research, assessed via Cronbach's alpha (Cronbach, 1951), with conventional thresholds of ≥ 0.70 acceptable and ≥ 0.80 good, though very high values (> 0.95) may signal redundant items; and inter-rater reliability for observational or coded data, quantified with Cohen's kappa or intraclass correlation. Crucially, reliability is necessary but not sufficient for validity — a flawed instrument can be perfectly consistent.

Validity: accuracy of measurement and inference

Validity concerns whether an instrument measures what it claims and whether the conclusions drawn are warranted. Following Cronbach & Meehl (1955) and the experimental tradition of Campbell & Stanley (1963), the principal types are:

  • Content validity — the items adequately sample the conceptual domain; usually judged by expert panels.
  • Construct validity — the instrument captures the intended theoretical construct, decomposed into convergent validity (correlates with measures of the same construct) and discriminant validity (diverges from unrelated constructs), classically tested via the multitrait–multimethod matrix (Campbell & Fiske, 1959).
  • Criterion validity — correlation with an external standard, split into concurrent (measured at the same time) and predictive (predicts a future outcome).
  • Internal validity — the strength of a causal claim; threatened by confounding variables, selection, history, maturation, and instrumentation effects.
  • External validity — the generalisability of findings to other people, settings, and times.

A perennial tension exists between internal and external validity: tightly controlled experiments maximise internal validity but, by stripping context, may sacrifice external validity — a trade-off central to the design choices of Lesson 4.

The reliability–validity relationship

The dartboard heuristic captures it: shots tightly clustered off-centre are reliable but invalid; shots scattered around the bullseye are neither reliably hitting nor practically valid; only a tight cluster on the bullseye is both reliable and valid. Formally, reliability sets a ceiling on validity — measurement error attenuates observed correlations — so reliability must be established first.

Trustworthiness: the qualitative analogue

Because reliability and validity are grounded in positivist assumptions, Lincoln & Guba (1985) reframed quality for interpretivist work under the umbrella of trustworthiness, with four criteria: credibility (analogue of internal validity — via prolonged engagement, triangulation, member checking), transferability (analogue of external validity — via thick description enabling readers to judge applicability), dependability (analogue of reliability — via an auditable, documented process), and confirmability (analogue of objectivity — findings grounded in data, not researcher bias, evidenced by an audit trail). This reframing aligns quality criteria with the philosophy chosen in Lesson 2.

Research ethics

Ethical conduct rests on principles codified across professional and institutional codes (e.g. the Belmont Report's respect for persons, beneficence, and justice). Operationally: informed consent — participants understand purpose, procedures, risks, and agree freely; voluntary participation and the right to withdraw without penalty; avoidance of harm — physical, psychological, social, and reputational, including for the researcher; confidentiality and anonymity — protecting identities and sensitive data; data protection — compliance with regimes such as GDPR governing storage, retention, and use; and research integrity — no fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism, and honest, complete reporting. Special care attends vulnerable populations and covert research, where deception must be justified and debriefing provided. Most institutions mandate ethics committee / Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval before data collection begins; proceeding without it can invalidate a study.

Worked example

A researcher develops a 10-item "managerial trust" instrument. Internal consistency is strong (Cronbach's alpha = 0.88). An expert panel confirms content validity. Construct validity is supported when the scale correlates r = 0.62 with an established engagement measure (convergent) yet only r = 0.09 with an unrelated numeracy test (discriminant). Test–retest over four weeks yields r = 0.81, confirming temporal stability. Ethically, the study secured IRB approval, used signed informed-consent forms detailing withdrawal rights, anonymised all responses with coded IDs, and stored data on an encrypted, access-controlled drive in line with GDPR. The methods chapter reports each figure explicitly — the standard expected by examiners and journal reviewers.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Conflating reliability with validity — the single most common error; a consistent measure can be consistently wrong.
  • Reporting only alpha as if it proved validity — internal consistency is one reliability index, not evidence of accuracy.
  • Ignoring discriminant validity — showing what a scale correlates with but not what it should not.
  • Treating ethics as a tick-box obtained after the fact rather than designed in from the start.
  • Assuming anonymity equals confidentiality — they are distinct protections.

Cross-links

This lesson assesses the quality of the measurement built in Data Collection & Measurement (Lesson 6) and the representativeness fixed in Sampling (Lesson 5). Its validity concepts — especially internal and external validity — connect to Research Design (Lesson 4), and its trustworthiness criteria to the philosophy of Lesson 2. The reliability and validity of the data ultimately condition the inferences permitted in Data Analysis (Lesson 8).
